question: Briefly explain the process of inserting a new node into a Binary Search Tree (BST). What is the key property that must be maintained during insertion?
expectedPoints: Start at the root node and compare the new value with the current node's value.
If the new value is less, go to the left subtree; if greater, go to the right subtree.
Repeat this process recursively or iteratively until an empty child pointer (null) is found, then insert the new node there.
The key property that must be maintained is the BST ordering: all values in the left subtree are less than the node, and all values in the right subtree are greater.

question: Compare and contrast the three standard tree traversal methods: In-order, Pre-order, and Post-order. For each, provide the order of node visitation and one common use case.
expectedPoints: In-order: Visits left subtree, then root, then right subtree. Use case: Outputs nodes of a BST in sorted, ascending order.
Pre-order: Visits root, then left subtree, then right subtree. Use case: Creating a copy of the tree or prefix notation for expression trees.
Post-order: Visits left subtree, then right subtree, then root. Use case: Deleting a tree or postfix notation for expression trees.
All are depth-first traversals and are typically implemented using recursion or a stack.

question: Describe a detailed algorithm to create a full, deep copy of a binary tree. Your answer should cover the traversal strategy, the copying process for each node, and explain why the chosen strategy is effective. Discuss the time and space complexity of your approach.
expectedPoints: The most natural algorithm uses a Pre-order (DFS) traversal strategy.

The process is recursive: start at the root, create a new node with the same data, then recursively copy the left subtree and assign it to the new node's left pointer, then recursively copy the right subtree and assign it to the new node's right pointer.
Pre-order is effective because it creates the parent node first, which allows us to then connect its copied children to it as the recursion unwinds.
Time Complexity is O(n) as every node in the original tree is visited exactly once.
Space Complexity is O(h) where h is the height of the tree, due to the recursion call stack. In the worst case (a skewed tree), this is O(n).
